_meta.description: Demand themes — the structural narratives driving the AI hardware supply chain. Each maps a supply-chain position list, an investment thesis, structure-vs-cycle scores (0-100), portfolio positioning tiers, and watch/risk.

items.why: Merchant GPU + custom ASIC — the demand engine
Every accelerator is a TSMC leading-edge wafer
The gating capacity constraint on shipments
HBM gates GPU output; ~8 stacks per GPU
Rack-scale integration of NVL72-class systems

themes.thesis: The single largest capex cycle in tech history. Hyperscalers + neoclouds + sovereign AI are spending ~$300-400B/yr, and demand still outruns supply at CoWoS and HBM. The durable winners own the bottlenecks (TSMC, SK Hynix) or the platform (Nvidia CUDA, Broadcom ASIC).
themes.risk: Capex digestion / ROI doubt is the key cycle risk — if hyperscaler returns disappoint, the whole stack de-rates. Watch hyperscaler capex guidance, inference-vs-training mix, and any pause in frontier-model spend.

themes.sub: HBM is the AI supply gate

items.why: SK Hynix ~62%; all sold out for 2026
TSV + hybrid bonding equipment
HBM4 logic base die pulls foundry into memory
HBM complexity lengthens test time

themes.thesis: HBM is the clearest hard bottleneck in AI — bandwidth, not FLOPS, increasingly gates training. A Korea-centric oligopoly with pricing power and multi-year sold-out books. HBM4 (2026) raises the technical bar and drops TSMC into the stack via the logic base die.
themes.risk: Memory is historically the most cyclical semi segment. A demand air-pocket or Samsung/CXMT supply surge could break HBM pricing. Watch HBM4 qualification timing and any conventional-DRAM glut bleeding into sentiment.

items.why: The gating bottleneck on AI GPU output
Overflow capacity + advanced packaging
ABF substrate + film constraints
Hybrid bonding enables 3D stacking

themes.thesis: As transistor scaling slows, performance increasingly comes from packaging — the new Moore's Law. CoWoS is the visible bottleneck; substrates and hybrid-bonding tools are the second-order chokepoints. A multi-year capacity-build story with value shifting front-end to back-end.
themes.risk: CoWoS capacity is being added aggressively — a swing from shortage to balance would compress the scarcity premium. Watch TSMC packaging capacity adds, FOPLP (panel-level) disruption, and substrate supply normalization.

themes.sub: Optics content per GPU rising fast

items.why: <5 makers; Nvidia spent ~$4B to lock supply
China ~63% assembly; Fabrinet diversified
The brains of the transceiver — US value capture
Co-packaged optics inflection on TSMC COUPE

themes.thesis: Optics is becoming the power and bandwidth bottleneck of AI networking, and content per GPU is scaling sharply (800G→1.6T). EML lasers are a genuine chokepoint; CPO (2026) moves optics onto the package and drops TSMC into photonics. US captures the silicon + laser value; China the assembly.
themes.risk: CPO timing/adoption is uncertain and could disrupt the pluggable-transceiver model. LPO (linear-drive) could bypass DSPs. Watch CPO ramp at Nvidia/Broadcom and any laser-capacity catch-up easing the EML squeeze.

items.why: Power + liquid cooling at rack scale
48V-to-chip for 1kW+ GPUs
Cold plates, CDUs; ~76% liquid by 2026
Gas turbines + nuclear PPAs (cross to energy)

themes.thesis: Power availability and heat rejection — not chips — are now the binding constraints on AI deployment. Liquid cooling jumps from ~15% to ~76% of the mix by 2026, CDU capacity is short, and grid/transformer lead times gate sites. The deepest cross-link from AI hardware into the energy supply chain.
themes.risk: A datacenter capex slowdown hits this directly. Cooling tech is fragmenting (cold plate vs immersion) — backing the wrong standard is a risk. Watch power-availability headlines, transformer lead times, and liquid-cooling attach rates.

items.why: Harmonic + RV gearing — costliest joint part
Roller screws + torque motors; a humanoid bottleneck
Sanhua exclusive Tier-1 Optimus assembler
Embodied-AI compute links to accelerators
Vision, lidar, force/torque for manipulation

themes.thesis: Humanoids create an entirely new precision-motion BOM. The supply chain — not the AI — is the hard part: reducers, roller screws and torque motors are Japanese/European chokepoints that China is racing to localize and cost-down. The clearest pure-plays sit in the actuator components, not the robot brands.
themes.risk: Timeline + unit-volume risk is high — humanoid deployment forecasts are speculative and could slip years. China cost-down could crush component margins. Watch Optimus/Figure production numbers and actual reducer/screw order flow vs hype.

items.why: OLEDoS/LCoS/microLED — make-or-break component
Getting the image to the eye in a glasses form
Substrate the waveguide is etched into
De-facto smart-glasses compute
Goertek light engines; Meta drives the BOM

themes.thesis: Meta's Ray-Ban breakout proved a consumer market for AI-camera glasses ahead of full AR. The two unsolved problems — micro-displays and waveguides — are deep chokepoints with no clear winner yet, which makes the component layer (displays, glass, SoC) more investable than the device brands.
themes.risk: Earliest-stage theme here — full AR remains years out and display/waveguide tech is unsettled (cost, yield, FOV). Most pure-plays are private (Lumus, JBD). Watch Meta/Apple device unit sales and any display-tech standardization.

items.why: CHIPS Act + EU Chips Act capacity
US-supply-security premium
National AI compute mandates
The parallel, sanctioned stack

themes.thesis: Governments now treat AI compute as strategic infrastructure, funding domestic fabs, packaging and sovereign clouds. This bifurcates the supply chain (a Western stack + a Chinese stack) and creates policy-driven demand and supply-security premia — but also subsidy-dependent overcapacity risk.
themes.risk: Subsidy-driven capacity can become overcapacity (esp. mature nodes); reshored fabs face cost/yield disadvantages. Policy can reverse (the May→July 2025 EDA on/off). Watch CHIPS disbursements, China curbs, and mature-node price wars.
